response: When I inherit a project, I start by reading whatever artifacts exist—tickets, docs, old decks. If nothing is written down, I treat the first week as \archaeology\" and document what I learn as I go."
I’m getting better at making my files readable: consistent naming, short notes at the top, and small checklists. It’s not fancy, but it prevents me from repeating the same work later.
The biggest gap for our team is turning insights into decisions. I’ve started writing a one-page decision note after research instead of a long slide deck, and it gets more follow-through.
I used AI to summarize interview transcripts, but I still review the raw quotes. The summaries are helpful for speed, but they sometimes miss the nuance that changes the interpretation.
Documentation matters most when handoffs happen. If the rationale isn’t captured, people rewrite the same text problems again and again, especially for error messages and microcopy.
I can’t understand code by just reading it, so I run it and inspect outputs. I write comments near the parts I don’t understand yet, so future me knows where to focus next time.
Recruiting is still the bottleneck. I’m experimenting with a shared participant tracker and templates for outreach emails so we don’t reinvent the process every study.
I’ve started adding a confidence level to research takeaways (high/medium/low). It helps stakeholders understand what’s solid versus what’s directional.
Stakeholder alignment is easier when I document constraints explicitly—timeline, technical limits, and what we’re not doing. People argue less when the trade-offs are written down.
A terminology audit sounds boring, but inconsistent labels confuse users. I’m pushing for a simple glossary doc so design and engineering can reuse the same words.
Synthesis is where participation drops. I’m trying an async approach: share a small set of clips, ask teammates to tag themes, then I consolidate and document the final map.
We don’t always have a researcher available, so I sometimes do interviews myself. Having a lightweight interview guide and a clear note-taking template makes the results more usable.
